channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Picks Nicole Saphier for Surgeon General After Withdrawing Casey Means Nomination
item.description: Dr. Means’s nomination had stalled in part over her views on vaccines. The president said he was instead nominating Dr. Nicole B. Saphier, a radiologist at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.media:credit: Alex Kent for The New York Times
item.media:description: The withdrawal of Dr. Casey Means’s nomination is bound to be a deep disappointment for the Make America Healthy Again movement.

item.title: Crop Undercount Raises Questions About Reliability of U.S.D.A. Data
item.description: Corn estimates were off by 4.5 million acres last year. A lack of survey responses, not job cuts, led to the miss, the Agriculture Department said.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Draper
item.media:credit: Kathryn Gamble for The New York Times
item.media:description: A cornfield in Earlham, Iowa, last fall. The Agriculture Department botched its estimate for the size of the 2025 corn crop.

item.title: The Vaccine Skeptic in Trump’s New C.D.C. Leadership Team
item.description: Dr. Sara Brenner is a physician, an F.D.A. official and a “MAHA mom” who has said people should not reflexively believe in the benefits of vaccines.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Elizabeth Frantz/Reuters
item.media:description: Dr. Sara Brenner fully embraced the Make America Healthy Again movement after being named the acting commissioner of the Food and Drug Administration, signing her first emails to the agency’s staff with the slogan.

item.title: How Trump’s Iran Blockade Is Complicating a High-Stakes Trip to China
item.description: If President Trump flies to China as planned in May, the primary topic will clearly be the rippling economic effects of a war that Beijing has made clear it viewed as unnecessary.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger and Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural in Tehran last week showing Iran controlling the Strait of Hormuz. China imports about a third of its oil and gas through the waterway.

item.title: Hegseth Says Iran Cease-Fire Stops Clock for Congressional Approval
item.description: The defense secretary testified on the eve of the 60-day mark of the war, a major statutory deadline for the president to withdraw forces or seek approval from Congress to continue the fight.
item.dc:creator: Megan Mineiro
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth testified for the second straight day on Capitol Hill on Thursday.

item.title: Iran’s Supreme Leader Signals Plan to Maintain Control Over Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei said in a statement that Iran would establish “new legal frameworks” for the Strait of Hormuz. He also said his country would retain its nuclear capabilities.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Iranians held photos of the country’s supreme leader, Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei, at a government-organized march in Tehran on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump May Pull Troops From Germany. That Isn’t as Scary as It Once Was.
item.description: Germany has hugely increased its military spending, aiming to be less dependent on Washington. Its support for U.S. attacks on Iran may also give it leverage.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley, Christopher F. Schuetze and Steven Erlanger
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Sean Gallup/Getty Images
item.media:description: American soldiers in Germany last month. On Wednesday, President Trump declared that the United States was “studying and reviewing the possible reduction of Troops in Germany.”

item.title: How the Voting Rights Decision May Block the Rise of Young Black Leaders
item.description: Black Democrats in the South already face steep challenges when seeking political office. But the Supreme Court’s ruling could be felt for a generation.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas
item.media:credit: Rory Doyle for The New York Times
item.media:description: Evan Turnage, a former congressional aide, recently challenged the veteran Democratic lawmaker Bennie Thompson in a Mississippi district vulnerable to being redrawn.

item.title: After Supreme Court Decision, Louisiana Weighs Redrawing House Maps
item.description: It was not immediately clear whether Gov. Jeff Landry, a Republican, would postpone the May 16 primary election for House races after the court ruled the state’s congressional districts unconstitutional.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Mark Schiefelbein/Associated Press
item.media:description: Gov. Jeff Landry of Louisiana, a Republican, outside the White House in 2025. Mr. Landry is weighing whether to suspend the state’s May 16 primary to redraw congressional maps.

item.title: Chief Justice Roberts Played the Long Game on Voting Rights
item.description: Since his early days as a lawyer and in his first years on the bench, the chief justice has worked to limit the force of the Voting Rights Act.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Ron Edmonds/Associated Press
item.media:description: John Roberts and other nominees to the federal bench with President George W. Bush in 2001.

item.title: Gov. Janet Mills Exits Maine Senate Race as an Insurgent Democrat Rises
item.description: Her withdrawal reflects the energy of the party’s left and voters’ unease with older candidates and paves the way for Graham Platner to challenge Senator Susan Collins in November.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer, Katie Glueck and Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Janet Mills of Maine was not able to match the fund-raising of Graham Platner, a progressive political newcomer, or his standing in polls of Maine Democrats.

item.title: Mills Exit Is a Blow to Schumer as Democrats Question His Strategy
item.description: Senator Chuck Schumer, the New York Democrat and minority leader, considered the Maine governor his top recruit for winning the majority. Critics said her collapse showed he is out of touch with the party’s voters.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Chuck Schumer, Democrat of New York and the minority leader, at the Capitol last month.

item.title: ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’ Review: A Rhapsody in Cerulean
item.description: In this sequel, Andy (Anne Hathaway) and Miranda (Meryl Streep) encounter a series of crises that set the stage for a larger, existential catastrophe.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: Top 10 Reasons We Love the Original ‘Devil Wears Prada’ Movie
item.description: The 2006 comedy is filled with moments big and small that have stood the test of time, thanks in no small part to Meryl Streep’s turn as Miranda Priestly.
item.dc:creator: Maya Phillips

item.title: Jeffrey Epstein’s Possible Suicide Note Hidden from Public View
item.description: An inmate said he discovered the note after Mr. Epstein was found injured in his jail cell, weeks before his death. It’s now locked in a courthouse.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Weiser, Steve Eder and Jan Ransom

item.title: Epstein Victims’ Pursuit of Justice Finds a New Venue: Albany, N.Y.
item.description: Two victims of Jeffrey Epstein will testify at the State Capitol next week in support of a bill that would enable them to seek punitive damages from his estate.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Oreskes
item.media:credit: Cindy Schultz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Zellnor Myrie is pushing a bill to make it possible for victims of Jeffrey Epstein to seek punitive damages from Mr. Epstein’s estate.

item.title: Behind Powell’s High-Stakes Decision to Stay at the Fed
item.description: Jerome H. Powell will remain a governor at the Federal Reserve after his term as chair ends, in a bid to guard against a further incursion by the Trump administration on the central bank’s independence.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jerome H. Powell on Wednesday during what is expected to be his final news conference as chair of the Federal Reserve.

item.title: U.S. Economy Grew 2 Percent in Early 2026 Even as War in Iran Began to Hit Energy Prices
item.description: Gross domestic product expanded at a 2 percent annual rate in the first three months of the year, a period including first weeks of conflict in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Talmon Joseph Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jon Cherry for The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. economy continued to prove resilient in the first three months of the year, even as energy prices soared in the early weeks of the war.

item.title: Oil Price Hits Wartime High Above $120 a Barrel as Iran War Standoff Continues
item.description: The longer the disruption to Middle East fuel supplies lasts, the risk grows that higher energy costs will feed into broader inflation that could dent economic growth.
item.dc:creator: Gregory Schmidt and Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Scott Olson/Getty Images

item.title: Is A.I. a Threat to Humanity? Not in This Trial.
item.description: One of Elon Musk’s abiding fears is that A.I. could one day threaten humans. But the jurors deciding his suit against OpenAI probably won’t hear about it.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Manuel Orbegozo/Reuters
item.media:description: Elon Musk returned to the witness stand in federal court for a third day on Thursday in what is expected to be a monthlong trial.

item.title: Inside the Courtroom at the OpenAI Trial
item.description: A New York Times tech reporter shares a dispatch from the press gallery of the showdown between Elon Musk and Sam Altman.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: A line to enter the Ronald V. Dellums U.S. Courthouse in Oakland, Calif., on Wednesday.

item.title: How A.I. Killed Student Writing (and Revived It)
item.description: High school and college teachers are watching students write, in the classroom, in order to protect against the incursion of artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Dana Goldstein and Lauren Lancaster
item.media:credit: Lauren Lancaster for The New York Times

item.title: Is Yoko Ono Still Our Most Radical Artist?
item.description: In the 1960s, she invited an audience to cut off her clothes. As attacks on women’s rights escalate, “Cut Piece” and other decades-old works of feminist art feel more relevant than ever.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Fortini

item.title: Camp Mystic Says It Will Not Reopen This Summer After Fatal Texas Floods
item.description: In a statement, the camp said it told Texas regulators that it was “withdrawing its application for a summer 2026 camp license” after catastrophic flooding last summer.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Camp Mystic, where 28 people died in catastrophic flooding last year, said on Thursday that it would not reopen this summer.

item.title: Smog in Phoenix and Salt Lake City? The E.P.A. Is Blaming Asia.
item.description: The Trump administration says the cities shouldn’t be penalized for unhealthy air because pollution can blow in from abroad. Some experts say that’s preposterous.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Matt York/Associated Press
item.media:description: Phoenix has long struggled with smog collecting in its bowl-shaped topography. The city failed to meet a federal air pollution standard in 2024.

item.title: High-Profile Squatting Incidents Are on the Rise Across the Country
item.description: States are responding to a rise in high-profile squatting cases, in luxury homes and public housing alike.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Nazaryan
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Police officers are often reluctant to get involved in what they deem a civil matter.

item.title: Three of Nashville’s Finest Writers Explain How Country Hits Get Written
item.description: Shane McAnally, Brandy Clark and Josh Osborne talk about songwriting as a day job and the art of telling simple truths in a single line.

item.title: How Taylor Swift Writes a Song, In Her Own Words
item.description: The artist shares stories behind some of her biggest hits, her love of a “rant bridge” and how life in the public eye informs the stories she tells in her songs.

item.title: Silicon Valley Is Bracing for a Permanent Underclass
item.description: The people building A.I. fear that we have only a short time before advanced A.I. disrupts the labor force.
item.dc:creator: Jasmine Sun

item.title: Why Are We Still Driving?
item.description: Confronting the weirdness of a Waymo future.
item.dc:creator: Ross Douthat and Sophia Alvarez Boyd
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: Senators Say Lawyers Helped Wealthy Clients Dodge Taxes in Puerto Rico
item.description: Rich Americans are writing off taxes they owed before they moved to Puerto Rico, the Senate Finance Committee says.
item.dc:creator: Jesse Drucker
item.media:credit: Xavier Garcia/Bloomberg
item.media:description: Dan Morehead, founder of the cryptocurrency investment firm Pantera Capital. Shortly after he relocated to Puerto Rico in 2021, his company sold a large position, generating capital gains of more than $1 billion.

item.title: Attacks on Jewish Targets in Europe Suggest Hybrid Warfare
item.description: Officials are investigating similar attacks across Europe, all claimed by a shadowy Islamist group that may be using low-cost, unsophisticated methods to sow fear in Jewish communities.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia and Koba Ryckewaert
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Henry Nicholls/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: An arson attack in the Golders Green neighborhood of London in March destroyed four ambulances run by a Jewish organization.

item.title: Mamdani Suggested UK Should Return the Koh-i-Noor Diamond. Here’s the Back Story.
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani of New York suggested that Britain should return the gem, a symbol of colonial plunder. Here’s the back story of the famed jewel.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon and Hari Kumar
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Tim Graham Photo Library, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The Koh-i-Noor diamond, right of center, is set in a crown made for the British royal family.

item.title: Venice Biennale Jury Resigns Amid Tension Over Awards Ban
item.description: The jury of the world’s most important art exhibition had said it wouldn’t consider artists from countries whose leaders are accused of crimes against humanity.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Stefano Mazzola/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Israel pavilion at the Venice Biennale in 2024.
